visuals.title: Random Access Protocols
visuals.description: Animated visualization showing the evolution and operation of random access protocols including ALOHA, CSMA, CSMA/CD, and CSMA/CA with station transmission behaviors and collision scenarios

diagrams.title: MAC Protocols Classification
diagrams.description: Hierarchical diagram showing the three main categories of Medium Access Control protocols: Controlled Access (Token Passing, Polling), Random Access (ALOHA, CSMA variants), and Channelization (FDMA, TDMA, CDMA)
diagrams.purpose: Provides structural overview of how random access protocols fit within the broader MAC protocol taxonomy, helping students understand the relationship between different access methods

diagrams.title: ALOHA Protocol Vulnerable Time
diagrams.description: Timeline comparison between Pure ALOHA and Slotted ALOHA showing frame transmission period T and vulnerable time. Pure ALOHA shows vulnerable time as 2T (before and after frame), while Slotted ALOHA shows reduced vulnerable time of T aligned to discrete time slots
diagrams.purpose: Illustrates the key improvement of Slotted ALOHA over Pure ALOHA by reducing the vulnerable time, explaining why slotted version achieves double the maximum throughput (36.8% vs 18.4%)

diagrams.title: CSMA Protocol Variants
diagrams.description: Comparison flowchart showing three CSMA approaches: 1-Persistent (transmits immediately when idle, high collision risk), Non-Persistent (waits random time before sensing, lower collisions but higher delay), and p-Persistent (used in slotted systems, transmits with probability p or defers)
diagrams.purpose: Demonstrates the differences between CSMA variants and their trade-offs between throughput and delay, helping students understand why different variants are suitable for different network conditions

diagrams.title: CSMA/CD Operation in Ethernet
diagrams.description: Sequence diagram showing Ethernet CSMA/CD process: 1) Sense channel idle, 2) Transmit frame while monitoring for collisions, 3) Detect collision, 4) Send jam signal, 5) Execute binary exponential backoff before retransmission
diagrams.purpose: Explains how Ethernet networks detect and handle collisions in wired environments, including the jam signal mechanism and binary exponential backoff algorithm used to resolve contention

diagrams.title: CSMA/CA in Wireless Networks
diagrams.description: Flowchart showing IEEE 802.11 WiFi collision avoidance mechanism including: DIFS wait, random backoff counter, optional RTS/CTS exchange, data frame transmission, and ACK acknowledgment
diagrams.purpose: Illustrates how wireless networks avoid collisions through CSMA/CA mechanisms including RTS/CTS handshake, which addresses the hidden node problem inherent in wireless media
